content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I still remember the day I left nursing.
There was a patient whose words and behavior slowly wore me down. I kept telling myself that I had to endure it.
“I’m a nurse. I should be stronger.”
“Everyone else is working hard, so I should too.”
I believed that quitting meant I had failed.
But one day, I reached my limit.
I loved being a nurse. I truly did. What I didn’t know at the time was that loving your job doesn’t mean you have to sacrifice yourself for it.
So I made a decision that felt impossible back then.
I stepped away from nursing.
At first, it felt like running away. I worried about what other people would think. I felt guilty. I wondered if I would regret my decision.
But looking back now, I can say this:
It was okay to leave.
Because when I stepped away, I finally saw a world that had been invisible to me.
I realized that nursing wasn’t the only path available to me.
I realized that I was allowed to protect my own heart.
And I realized that I had more possibilities than I had ever imagined.
Today, I still work as a nurse – but only once a week.
For me, it became the perfect balance.
I didn’t have to choose between being a nurse forever or quitting completely. I found my own way of staying connected to the profession while also making room for other parts of myself.
And those other parts have changed my life.
Today, I run my own small business. I create content. I explore new ideas. And surprisingly, I’m writing a book with the help of AI.
If someone had told the old version of me that one day I would be using AI to write a book, I never would have believed them.
But life can change in ways we never expect.
The experiences that hurt us can become the stories that help someone else.
That’s why I’m writing this book.
I’m writing about the painful experiences I had as a nurse – not because I want to stay in the pain, but because I hope my story reaches someone who is struggling right now.
Someone who feels trapped.
Someone who feels guilty for wanting to leave.
Someone who believes they have no other options.
If that’s you, I want you to know this:
You don’t have to keep suffering just because you’re a nurse.
You don’t have to stay in a place that is breaking you.
It’s okay to pause.
It’s okay to step away.
It’s okay to choose yourself.
Because on the other side of that decision, there may be a life you cannot even imagine yet.
I know that because I found mine.
And now, I’m turning that journey into a book – hoping it becomes the sentence that someone else needs to read:
“It’s okay to leave. Your life doesn’t end there. Sometimes, it’s where a new one begins.”
